retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, DNA Packaging -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The nucleosome structure was first described in 1974 by Roger Kornberg who, with evidence from biochemical experiments, X-ray diffraction studies, and electron microscopy images, proposed the nucleosome was comprised of repeating units of eight histone proteins and about 200 DNA base pairs. [4] In the same year, Markus Noll gave a visually interpretable result to understand how DNA wraps around the nucleosomes. [5] The results of his experiments showed that DNA wrapped was cleaved when exposed to DNAse I, which suggested that the enzyme must have had access to the DNA. The results of this experiment favored the theory that DNA was wound on the outside of the nucleosome unit and supported the nucleosome structure proposed by Kornberg, with each nucleosome consisting of approximately 200 DNA base pairs.

retrieved_docs.title: Nuclear Medicine Instrumentation -- Clinical Significance -- Probe Counting Systems
retrieved_docs.content: Miniature gamma-ray probes: These probes are specialized tools primarily used in oncological applications, particularly for sentinel lymph node scintigraphy and radio-guided surgery. Given their role in surgical procedures, these probes must be highly efficient in detecting gamma radiation and easily maneuverable during surgery. [26] The probe comprises a 10-mm-thick CsI(Tl) scintillation crystal paired with a silicone photodiode. This choice of a silicon photodiode is made to replace the traditional PMT due to its lightweight properties and compatibility with the CsI(Tl) scintillation crystal, making the probe more convenient for surgical use.

retrieved_docs.title: Nuclear Medicine Instrumentation -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Nuclear medicine emerged in the late 19th century with the discovery of radioactivity, which is now widely used in both diagnostic imaging and therapeutics. [1] A vital tool in nuclear medicine is the gamma camera, sometimes referred to as the scintillation camera (see Image. Gamma Camera). In addition, other critical instruments used in nuclear medicine include positron emission tomography (PET) cameras, single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) systems, and various detectors and counters.

retrieved_docs.title: Nuclear Medicine Instrumentation -- Clinical Significance -- Dose Calibrators
retrieved_docs.content: Within the dose calibrator, a sealed chamber contains argon and halogen gases maintained at high pressure ranging from 5 to 12 atm. Radiopharmaceuticals placed in a well surrounded by the ionization chamber generate varying currents dependent on the type and energy of radiation they emit. [21] Moreover, the dose calibrator features practical elements such as push buttons, settings to accommodate various radiopharmaceuticals, and an array of activity display units to facilitate accurate measurements.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Function -- MicroRNA
retrieved_docs.content: MicroRNAs (miRNA) are non-coding RNAs mainly involved in gene regulation. They are mostly processed from introns and are transcribed into primary miRNA from the host gene by RNA polymerase II. They are then modified by endonucleases, such as Drosha and Dicer, into mature miRNA. Studies have shown that miRNAs that bind to an untranslated region (3’UTR) on mRNAs suppress translation, while miRNA binding to promoter regions can upregulate transcription. [13] miRNAs can also function similarly to hormones. They are released into the extracellular fluid and taken up by target cells for regulation of cellular activity. Additionally, researchers are studying these extracellular miRNAs as ideal biomarkers for various diseases. Research has already shown circulating miRNAs to be involved in cancer through their role in controlling oncogenes and tumor suppressors. [14]

In addition, therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine is placed under radiation therapy, reflecting its role in both diagnostic imaging with radioactive tracers and therapeutic use of ionizing radiation. Together, these relationships emphasize that radionuclide localization of tumor is both a specialized diagnostic procedure within nuclear medicine and is performed through the application of radionuclide imaging techniques.

This integrated framework clarifies how diagnostic nuclear‑medicine modalities and therapeutic radiation practices are interrelated, supporting comprehensive patient evaluation and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1537
retrieved_docs.content: Other nuclear medicine imaging serves as an overarching category for radionuclide‑based diagnostic techniques. Within this category, the Radioisotope study of central nervous system is a specialized imaging modality that focuses on evaluating neurological structures using radioactive tracers. This CNS‑focused study further includes a specific application, the Radionuclide identification of eye tumor, which utilizes similar radionuclide principles to detect and characterize ocular neoplasms. In addition, Radionuclide imaging – action is described as a method employed in other nuclear medicine imaging, indicating that the procedural actions used in radionuclide imaging are integral to the broader suite of nuclear medicine examinations. Together, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical organization in which general nuclear medicine imaging methods give rise to organ‑specific studies and targeted diagnostic actions, supporting comprehensive patient assessment and tailored clinical care.
